title: ساقی نامہ
description: One of Iqbal's grandest nazms. Cast in the classical 'saqi nama' form (an address to the cup-bearer), it opens with the rebirth of spring in the mountains, then turns into a sweeping meditation on the upheavals of the modern age, the decline of the Muslim world, the secret of life as ceaseless motion, and above all the doctrine of khudi (selfhood). Iqbal asks the Saqi for the wine of spiritual awakening that will set reason free and rekindle the dead fire of love.

couplets.transliteration: Hua khema-zan kaarvaan-e bahaar / Iram ban gaya daaman-e kohsaar
couplets.translation: The caravan of spring has pitched its tents; the foot of the mountains has turned into the garden of Iram (paradise).
couplets.explanation: Iqbal opens with the arrival of spring imagined as a great caravan that has halted and pitched camp across the land. The mountainside, now in full bloom, has become Iram, the fabled paradise-garden of Arab legend. Together the two lines establish the poem's setting: a world reborn in colour and life, a fitting backdrop against which the poet will soon ask the cup-bearer for the wine of spiritual renewal.

couplets.transliteration: Gul o nargis o sosan o nastaran / Shaheed-e azal laala-e khooneen-kafan
couplets.translation: The rose, narcissus, lily and wild rose are in bloom; the tulip is a martyr of eternity, wrapped in a blood-stained shroud.
couplets.explanation: The poet names the flowers of spring one by one, then fixes on the red tulip (laala). Its deep crimson makes Iqbal call it a 'martyr of eternity' draped in a blood-soaked shroud, a martyr fallen since the beginning of time. Read together, the couplet moves from simple catalogue of beauty to a charged image: even amid the joy of spring there is a hint of sacrifice and passion, a theme of love-unto-death that recurs throughout the poem.

couplets.transliteration: Jahaan chhup gaya parda-e rang mein / Lahu ki hai gardish rag-e sang mein
couplets.translation: The world has hidden itself behind a veil of colour; even in the vein of stone, blood is now coursing.
couplets.explanation: Spring's abundance is so intense that the ordinary world has vanished behind a 'veil of colour'. In the second line Iqbal pushes the image further: life now pulses even through the veins of stone, as if blood circulates in the rocks themselves. Taken together, the couplet conveys that the life-force of spring has penetrated everything, even the seemingly lifeless, a prelude to the poem's central vision of life as a force in restless, universal motion.

couplets.transliteration: Fizaa neeli neeli hava mein suroor / Thahirte naheen aashiyaan mein tuyoor
couplets.translation: The sky is a deep blue and the breeze is laden with ecstasy; the birds cannot stay still within their nests.
couplets.explanation: Iqbal paints the sensory intoxication of spring: the azure sky and a wind so heady that it stirs joy in every living thing. The birds, unable to remain in their nests, embody the restless impulse of life awakened by the season, a motion that, in Iqbal's vision, mirrors the soul's irrepressible urge toward flight and self-realization.

words.meaning: stream, rivulet (joo-e kohistaan: mountain stream, izafat)

couplets.transliteration: Voh joo-e kohistaan uchakti hui / Atakti lachakti sarakti hui
couplets.translation: Behold that mountain stream, leaping along, catching, swaying, and slipping as it goes.
couplets.explanation: Iqbal turns to the mountain brook, whose every motion he renders through a cascade of rhyming present participles. The piling of verbs, leaping, snagging, bending, sliding, enacts in sound the lively, dancing energy of running water, an extended image of dynamic, ceaseless life that Iqbal prizes over inert stillness.

couplets.transliteration: Uchhalti phisalti sanbhalti hui / Bare pech khaa kar nikalti hui
couplets.translation: Bounding, slipping, then steadying itself, winding through many a twist before it breaks free.
couplets.explanation: The portrait of the stream continues with the same musical rush of participles: the water bounds, slips, recovers, and threads through countless coils before emerging into the open. Iqbal makes the brook's struggle through obstacles a metaphor for life's onward thrust, repeatedly checked yet always recovering its balance and forcing a passage forward.

words.meaning: to split, tear (cheer detii: rends apart)
to split, tear (cheer detii: rends apart)

couplets.transliteration: Ruke jab to sil cheer detii hai yeh / Pahaaron ke dil cheer detii hai yeh
couplets.translation: When it is held back, it cleaves the very rock; this stream rends apart the heart of the mountains.
couplets.explanation: When obstructed, the stream does not surrender but splits the stone and tears through the mountains' core. Here Iqbal's hydraulic image becomes overtly philosophical: resistance only intensifies the force of life. It anticipates his doctrine of khudi (selfhood), in which the self that meets opposition does not yield but grows mightier, carving its own path through every barrier.

couplets.transliteration: Zaraa dekh ai saaqi-e laalah-faam / Sunaatii hai yeh zindagi ka payaam
couplets.translation: Just look, O tulip-hued cupbearer; this stream proclaims the message of life.
couplets.explanation: Iqbal now addresses the Saqi, the rosy-cheeked cupbearer who gives the poem its title, bidding him witness the torrent. The rushing water is no mere scene of beauty but a herald 'proclaiming the message of life': the gospel of struggle, motion, and self-assertion. The address to the Saqi turns the nature description into the prelude of a prayer for a transformative wine of the spirit.

words.meaning: give (completes pilaa de: pour out for me)
burning (pardah-soz: that burns away the veil)
season (fasl-e gul: season of flowers, izafat)
day (roz roz: every day, day after day)

couplets.transliteration: Pilaa de mujhe voh mai-e pardah-soz / Keh aatii naheen fasl-e gul roz roz
couplets.translation: Pour me that wine which burns away every veil, for the season of roses does not come every day.
couplets.explanation: The poet asks the Saqi for the 'veil-burning wine', a Sufi-tinged symbol of mystical insight and self-awakening that consumes the curtains hiding ultimate reality. He justifies the urgency by noting that 'the season of roses does not come every day': spring, and the rare moment of spiritual openness it represents, is fleeting and must be seized at once.

words.meaning: inner conscience, soul (zameer-e hayaat: the soul of life, izafat)
intoxication, ecstasy (masti-e kaainaat: rapture of the cosmos, izafat)

couplets.transliteration: Voh mai jis se raushan zameer-e hayaat / Voh mai jis se hai masti-e kaainaat
couplets.translation: That wine by which the inner soul of life is illumined; that wine which is the very intoxication of the cosmos.
couplets.explanation: Iqbal extols the wine he craves through parallel definitions: it is the spirit that lights up the inner conscience of life and the rapture animating the whole universe. The repeated 'voh mai jis se' (that wine by which) builds an incantatory rhythm, identifying this wine not with a literal drink but with the divine creative ardor, ishq, love-energy, that gives existence its glow and motion.

words.meaning: harmony, music, melody (soz o saaz: passion and harmony)
secret, mystery (raaz-e azal: secret of eternity, izafat)

couplets.transliteration: Voh mai jis mein hai soz o saaz-e azal / Voh mai jis se khulta hai raaz-e azal
couplets.translation: That wine which holds the passion and harmony of eternity; that wine by which the secret of the primordial beginning is unveiled.
couplets.explanation: The catalogue of the wine's powers reaches its climax: it contains 'soz o saaz-e azal', the burning ardor and the ordering melody present at creation's eternal origin, and it unlocks 'raaz-e azal', the mystery of the beginningless beginning. Iqbal fuses the wine-symbol with the Quranic resonance of pre-eternity, suggesting that true selfhood drinks from the same primordial fire and music out of which the cosmos itself was sung into being.

words.meaning: set to fight, pit (laraa de: make them clash)
royal falcon, the king of hawks

couplets.transliteration: Uthaa saaqiyaa pardah is raaz se / Laraa de mamoole ko shahbaaz se
couplets.translation: O cupbearer, lift the veil from this secret; pit the little wagtail against the royal falcon.
couplets.explanation: Iqbal beseeches the Saqi to unveil the mystery and, through the wine of selfhood, to 'pit the wagtail against the falcon', to so empower the weak and humble that they can stand against the mighty. The proverbial image celebrates khudi's transformative force: when the self is kindled, even the frailest creature acquires the daring to challenge the strongest, overturning every settled hierarchy of power.

words.meaning: of the age, of the times (zamaane ke: the age's)

couplets.transliteration: Zamaane ke andaaz badle gaye / Nayaa raag hai saaz badle gaye
couplets.translation: The ways of the age have changed; there is a new tune now, and the instruments themselves have been changed.
couplets.explanation: The poem pivots from cosmic prayer to contemporary history. Iqbal announces that the temper of the modern age has utterly shifted, there is 'a new tune', and even 'the instruments' have been replaced, signaling that the old order's very mechanisms of culture and power are obsolete. The musical metaphor frames the upheavals of the early twentieth century as a fundamental change of melody to which humanity must now attune itself.

words.meaning: manner, way (is tarah: in such a way)
secret (raaz-e farang: the secret of the West, izafat)
Europe, the West, the Frankish lands
player, juggler (sheesha-baaz-e farang: the West's illusionist)

couplets.transliteration: Hua is tarah faash raaz-e farang / Keh hairat mein hai sheesha-baaz-e farang
couplets.translation: The secret of the West has been laid so bare that the West's own glass-juggler stands astonished.
couplets.explanation: Iqbal declares that the hidden machinery of European dominance has now been exposed for all to see, so completely that even the 'sheesha-baaz', the West's own master illusionist who once dazzled the world with his sleight of hand, is left dumbfounded. The image suggests that the colonial and capitalist conjuring trick of the West has lost its mystique, its spell broken once the subject peoples have seen through it.

words.meaning: politics, statecraft (siyaasat-gari: the craft of politics)
the earth, the land (here: the people of the world)
king, sultan (meer o sultaan: lords and monarchs)

couplets.transliteration: Puraani siyaasat-gari khwaar hai / Zameen meer o sultaan se be-zaar hai
couplets.translation: The old art of statecraft lies disgraced; the earth is weary of its lords and monarchs.
couplets.explanation: Continuing his survey of the collapsing old order, Iqbal proclaims that the time-worn craft of political manipulation has been shamed and that the very earth, the mass of humanity, has grown sick of its princes and kings. The couplet captures the revolutionary mood of the age following the First World War, when monarchies fell and faith in inherited authority crumbled, clearing the ground for the new tune announced earlier.

words.meaning: era, age, epoch (daur-e sarmaayah-daari: the age of capitalism, izafat)
is gone (repeated for emphasis: gone, gone)

couplets.transliteration: Gaya daur-e sarmaayah-daari gaya / Tamaasha dikhaa kar madaari gaya
couplets.translation: The age of capitalism is gone, gone for good; the showman has done his act and slipped away.
couplets.explanation: Iqbal pronounces, with emphatic repetition, the passing of the capitalist era, likening it to a street conjurer (madaari) who, after performing his crowd-pleasing tricks, has packed up and vanished. Written in the wake of the post-war economic crises, the couplet voices Iqbal's conviction, sympathetic to the upheavals of his time, that the exploitative system of capital was a passing illusion whose spell, like the magician's, had finally broken.

words.meaning: the Chinese, the people of China
to recover, to rouse, to come to themselves
to gush forth, to boil over

couplets.transliteration: Garaan-khwaab cheeni sanbhalne lage / Himaalah ke chashme ubalne lage
couplets.translation: The deep-slumbering Chinese have begun to stir awake; the springs of the Himalaya have begun to gush forth.
couplets.explanation: Turning to Asia, Iqbal hails the awakening of the East: the long 'deep-sleeping' Chinese are rousing themselves, and the Himalayan springs are bursting into flood, an image of fresh, irrepressible energy released from the heart of Asia. The couplet expresses Iqbal's hope that the colonized peoples of the East, like the snows feeding the great rivers, are stirring to new life and movement in the changed age.

words.meaning: Mount Tur (Sinai), where Moses saw the divine light
Faran — the mountain near Mecca associated with prophetic revelation
the divine effulgence, the manifestation of God's light
Kalim — Moses, 'the one who spoke with God'

couplets.transliteration: Dil-e Toor-e Seena o Faaraan do-neem / Tajalli ka phir muntazir hai Kaleem
couplets.translation: The heart of Sinai and of Faran is cleft in two; once again Moses stands awaiting the divine effulgence.
couplets.explanation: Iqbal invokes the sacred mountains, Sinai, where Moses received the Law, and Faran, linked to the rise of the final Prophet, describing their hearts as split asunder in longing. He pictures Kalim (Moses) again waiting for the tajalli, the blinding manifestation of God's light, suggesting that the awakening age is once more ripe for revelation and that a renewed spiritual dawn is imminent for the world and for Islam.

words.meaning: until, up to (abhi tak: still up to now)
the sacred thread / girdle worn by non-Muslims (symbol of unbelief)
wearing, clad in (zunnaar-posh: girt with the thread of disbelief)

couplets.transliteration: Musalmaan hai tauheed mein garm-josh / Magar dil abhi tak hai zunnaar-posh
couplets.translation: The Muslim is fervent in professing God's oneness, yet his heart still wears the girdle of unbelief.
couplets.explanation: Iqbal closes this movement with a piercing self-critique of the Muslim world. Outwardly the Muslim proclaims tauheed, the oneness of God, with zeal, 'but his heart still wears the zunnaar', the thread worn by non-believers, symbol of hidden idolatry and inner faithlessness. The couplet exposes the gap between profession and inner reality: until the heart is purified of its secret idols, the awakening of the age cannot truly redeem the Muslim.

couplets.transliteration: Tamaddun, tasawwuf, shari'at, kalam / Butan-e-ajam ke pujari tamam
couplets.translation: Civilization, Sufism, religious law, theology, / all of them have become worshippers of the idols of Persia.
couplets.explanation: Iqbal indicts the whole apparatus of Muslim intellectual and spiritual life, its culture, mysticism, jurisprudence, and dialectical theology, as having been corrupted by alien (ajami) influences. The 'idols of Persia' symbolize the speculative philosophy and life-denying mysticism that, in Iqbal's view, displaced the living, dynamic faith of early Islam, reducing it to imported abstraction.

couplets.transliteration: Haqiqat khurafat mein kho ga'i / Yeh ummat riwayat mein kho ga'i
couplets.translation: The Truth has been lost amid superstitions; / this community has been lost amid hollow traditions.
couplets.explanation: Iqbal mourns that the essential reality of Islam has been buried under layers of myth and superstition, while the ummah clings to inherited, second-hand reports rather than the living truth. The parallel structure of the two lines drives home that both the faith's substance and its bearers have surrendered the vital to the inert and the formulaic.

couplets.transliteration: Lubhata hai dil ko kalam-e-khatib / Magar lazzat-e-shauq se be-nasib
couplets.translation: The preacher's discourse beguiles the heart, / but it is bereft of the delight of true yearning.
couplets.explanation: Iqbal turns to the religious orator whose eloquence charms his listeners yet lacks the inner fire of ardent longing (shauq) for the Divine. The preacher's words please the ear but cannot kindle the soul, because rhetoric without the lived ecstasy of love is empty performance.

couplets.transliteration: Bayan us ka mantiq se suljha hua / Lughat ke bakheron mein uljha hua
couplets.translation: His exposition is smoothed out by logic, / yet snarled up in the squabbles of vocabulary.
couplets.explanation: Continuing his critique of the preacher and scholar, Iqbal notes the paradox that the discourse is logically polished but hopelessly entangled in pedantic disputes over words and definitions. The wordplay between suljha (untangled) and uljha (entangled) underscores that mere logical and lexical dexterity cannot deliver living truth; it only ensnares the spirit in barren technicality.

couplets.transliteration: Woh sufi keh tha khidmat-e-haq mein mard / Muhabbat mein yakta, hamiyyat mein fard
couplets.translation: That Sufi who was a true man in the service of Truth, / unrivalled in love and peerless in zealous honor
couplets.explanation: Iqbal recalls the ideal of the early Sufi: a heroic figure devoted wholly to God's service, supreme in love and unmatched in protective zeal for the faith. This idealized portrait is set up deliberately so that the following couplet can mourn how far the latter-day mystic has fallen from it.

couplets.transliteration: Ajam ke khayalat mein kho gaya / Yeh salik maqamat mein kho gaya
couplets.translation: He was lost in the speculations of Persia; / this wayfarer was lost amid the spiritual stations.
couplets.explanation: Completing the previous couplet's lament, Iqbal shows that the once-heroic Sufi degenerated by drowning in alien Persian metaphysics and in the abstract cartography of mystical 'stations.' The wayfarer (salik), meant to journey toward God through love and action, instead lost himself in self-absorbed theosophy, mistaking the map for the road.

couplets.transliteration: Bujhi ishq ki aag, andher hai / Musalman nahin, rakh ka dher hai
couplets.translation: The fire of love is extinguished, what darkness! / The Muslim is no Muslim now, but a heap of ashes.
couplets.explanation: Here Iqbal reaches the climax of his lament: with the flame of ishq put out, only darkness remains, and the Muslim has been reduced from a living blaze to a cold pile of ashes. For Iqbal, passionate love (ishq) is the very soul of faith and of selfhood (khudi); without it, the believer is a burnt-out residue, alive in name but dead in spirit.

couplets.transliteration: Sharab-e-kuhan phir pila, saqiya / Wahi jam gardish mein la, saqiya
couplets.translation: Pour out the old wine once more, O Saqi; / set that same cup circling again, O Saqi.
couplets.explanation: Iqbal now turns from lament to invocation, addressing the Saqi (the divine cup-bearer of grace) to serve again the 'old wine', the original, intoxicating spirit of faith and love that animated the first Muslims. The image of the cup circling once more is a prayer for revival, asking that the ancient ecstasy be restored to a community grown cold.

couplets.transliteration: Mujhe ishq ke par laga kar ura / Meri khak jugnu bana kar ura
couplets.translation: Fix upon me the wings of love and let me soar; / turn my dust into a firefly and make it fly.
couplets.explanation: The poet pleads with the Saqi to lend him the wings of ishq so that he may rise above his earthbound state. The wish to become a firefly transforms his lowly clay into something self-luminous and airborne, an emblem of a self (khudi) kindled by love that radiates its own inner light rather than merely reflecting another's.

couplets.transliteration: Khirad ko ghulami se azad kar / Jawanon ko piron ka ustad kar
couplets.translation: Set reason free from its bondage; / make the young the teachers of the old.
couplets.explanation: Iqbal asks the Saqi to liberate the intellect from servile imitation so that thought may serve the free, love-guided self rather than enslave it. His startling wish to make the young the teachers of their elders calls for a generation so revitalized by ishq and khudi that it surpasses the stagnant wisdom of the past and leads the renewal of the community.

couplets.transliteration: Hari shakh-e-millat tere nam se hai / Nafas is badan mein tere dam se hai
couplets.translation: The branch of the community stays green by your moisture; / the breath in this body lives by your life.
couplets.explanation: Addressing the Saqi (and ultimately the divine source of grace), Iqbal confesses total dependence: the community is a living branch kept verdant only by the cup-bearer's nourishing dew. The second line extends the image to the individual body, whose very breath is sustained by the Saqi's life-giving breath, an acknowledgment that both nation and self draw their vitality from this gift of love.

words.meaning: to writhe, to throb with longing

couplets.transliteration: Tarapne phadakne ki taufiq de / Dil-e-Murtaza, soz-e-Siddiq de
couplets.translation: Grant me the grace to throb and quiver with longing; / give me the heart of Murtaza, the burning ardor of Siddiq.
couplets.explanation: Iqbal prays for the restlessness of ardent love, the very throbbing and quivering that signal a heart alive with desire for God. He invokes two supreme exemplars: the valiant, love-filled heart of Ali (Murtaza) and the consuming spiritual fervor of Abu Bakr (Siddiq), asking that the youth be reanimated with the passion of these foundational figures of Islam.

couplets.transliteration: Jigar se wahi tir phir par kar / Tamanna ko sinon mein bedar kar
couplets.translation: Drive that same arrow through the vitals once more; / awaken longing within the breasts.
couplets.explanation: The 'arrow' is the wounding shaft of divine love that pierces the seat of feeling (jigar), and Iqbal begs the Saqi to let it strike again as it once did. The painful wound is welcomed because it rouses tamanna, the burning aspiration and desire that Iqbal sees as essential to a living heart and to the awakening of the community.

couplets.transliteration: Tere asmanon ke taron ki khair / Zaminon ke shab-zinda-daron ki khair
couplets.translation: May blessing be upon the stars of your heavens, / and upon the night-vigil keepers of the earth.
couplets.explanation: Iqbal offers a benediction in parallel: blessings on the stars that adorn the heavens, and on the 'night-keepers' (shab-zinda-daran), the devout who spend their nights awake in prayer and remembrance. By pairing celestial lights with earthly worshippers, he suggests that the wakeful lovers of God on earth are as luminous and worthy of protection as the stars above.

couplets.transliteration: Jawanon ko soz-e-jigar bakhsh de / Mera ishq, meri nazar bakhsh de
couplets.translation: Bestow upon the youth the burning of the heart; / grant them my love and my vision.
couplets.explanation: Iqbal asks the Saqi to gift the young the inner fire (soz-e-jigar) that marks a soul alive with longing. In a striking act of self-giving, he offers his own ishq and his own penetrating insight (nazar) to the coming generation, wishing them not just feeling but the visionary clarity that love confers, the union of passion and perception at the core of his philosophy of selfhood.

couplets.transliteration: Meri nao girdab se par kar / Yeh sabit hai to us ko sayyar kar
couplets.translation: Carry my boat safely across the whirlpool; / if it stands still, then set it moving.
couplets.explanation: Iqbal closes this passage with a personal prayer to be delivered through the whirlpool of his age's confusion. Borrowing the astronomical contrast of sabit (the 'fixed' stars) and sayyar (the wandering planets), he begs that if his being has grown stagnant it be set in motion, for in Iqbal's thought, ceaseless striving and movement, not static repose, are the signs of a truly living self.

couplets.transliteration: bata mujh ko asraar-e marg o hayaat / ke teri nigaahon mein hai kaainaat
couplets.translation: Reveal to me the secrets of death and life, for the whole universe lies within your gaze.
couplets.explanation: The poet turns directly to the Saqi, his spiritual guide, imploring him to unveil the mysteries of mortality and existence. The plea is grounded in a claim of the Saqi's all-encompassing vision: in his sight the entire cosmos is held, so he alone can disclose what death and life conceal. This opens the great metaphysical petition that drives the rest of the poem.

couplets.transliteration: mere deeda-e tar ki be-khwaabiyaan / mere dil ki poshida betaabiyaan
couplets.translation: The sleepless vigils of my tear-soaked eye, the hidden restlessnesses of my heart.
couplets.explanation: Here the poet begins to lay out the inventory of his inner treasures, the offerings of a faqir who possesses nothing material. He names the wakeful nights of his weeping eyes and the secret anguish concealed within his heart. These are not weaknesses but the marks of a soul perpetually yearning, the very capital of his spiritual life.

couplets.transliteration: mere naala-e neem-shab ka niyaaz / meri khalwat o anjuman ka gudaaz
couplets.translation: The humble devotion of my midnight lament, the tender melting of my solitude and my company.
couplets.explanation: The catalogue of the poet's spiritual wealth continues with the supplicating cry he raises in the dead of night, an act of pure niyaaz, self-effacing devotion before God. He adds the softening warmth that infuses both his moments alone and his moments among others. Whether in seclusion or society, his being is dissolved in longing, a state the faqir prizes above all riches.

couplets.transliteration: umangen meri aarzooen meri / umeeden meri justujooen meri
couplets.translation: My ardent yearnings and my desires, my hopes and my unending quests.
couplets.explanation: In a sweeping, rhythmically insistent line the poet piles up the dynamic forces of his soul, the enthusiasms, longings, hopes and searchings that keep him forever in motion. Each phrase ends with the possessive 'meri' (mine), emphasizing that these striving impulses are his alone, the authentic possessions of his selfhood. This restless desiring is, for Iqbal, the engine of khudi, the self that grows by ceaseless aspiration.

couplets.transliteration: meri fitrat aaina-e rozgaar / ghazaalaan-e afkaar ka marghzaar
couplets.translation: My nature is a mirror of the age, a meadow where the gazelles of thought roam free.
couplets.explanation: The poet describes his innate temperament as a mirror reflecting the whole condition of his era, so that the world's truths are imaged within him. He then likens his mind to a green pasture across which the gazelles of thought bound and graze, a vivid figure for the swift, graceful play of ideas in him. Together the images present the poet's inner life as both a faithful reflection of reality and a fertile ground teeming with living thought.

couplets.transliteration: mera dil meri razm-gaah-e hayaat / gumaanon ke lashkar yaqeen ka sabaat
couplets.translation: My heart is the very battlefield of life, the armies of doubt and the firm resolve of certainty.
couplets.explanation: The poet now offers his heart, calling it the arena where life's deepest struggle is fought out. Upon this battlefield the massed legions of doubt (gumaan) clash against the immovable steadfastness of faith and certainty (yaqeen). The couplet captures the central spiritual drama of Iqbal's thought, in which the self is forged through the perpetual contest between skepticism and conviction.

couplets.transliteration: yahi kuchh hai saaqi mataa-e faqeer / isi se faqeeri mein hoon main ameer
couplets.translation: This, O Saqi, is the whole wealth of a mendicant; by this alone, in my poverty I am rich.
couplets.explanation: The poet sums up the preceding inventory, declaring that these inner states, his sleepless longing, devotion and embattled heart, are the only possessions of the faqir. Yet this is precisely the paradox of faqr in Iqbal: spiritual poverty that renounces the world becomes a source of inexhaustible riches. Owning nothing material, the mendicant is made wealthy by the boundless treasures of his soul.

couplets.transliteration: mere qaafile mein luta de ise / luta de thikaane laga de ise
couplets.translation: Scatter this wealth among my caravan; lavish it freely, put it to its rightful use.
couplets.explanation: Having declared his inner riches, the poet now begs the Saqi not to hoard them but to distribute them throughout his 'caravan,' the community or nation journeying with him. The doubled imperative 'luta de' (squander it away) insists that true wealth is realized only when spent for others, a hallmark of Iqbalian faqr. The line transforms private spiritual gain into a gift for the collective, urging that it be 'put to its rightful place' in service of the millat.

couplets.transliteration: dama-dam ravaan hai yamm-e zindagi / har ek shai se paida ram-e zindagi
couplets.translation: Moment by moment flows the ocean of life; from every single thing its restless motion is revealed.
couplets.explanation: Here begins Iqbal's great meditation on zindagi, life conceived not as a static substance but as a river or sea in perpetual flow, surging onward breath by breath. The second line universalizes this: every existing thing manifests 'ram,' the ceaseless, fleeting movement that is the essence of the living cosmos. For Iqbal, to be is to move, and reality itself is dynamic process rather than fixed being.

couplets.transliteration: isi se hui hai badan ki namood / ke shole mein poshida hai mauj-e dood
couplets.translation: From this very motion the body took its form, just as within the flame a wave of smoke lies concealed.
couplets.explanation: The poet explains that the visible material body itself arose out of life's restless movement, the same dynamic principle described in the previous couplet. He illustrates with a luminous image: just as smoke is latently coiled within fire and rises from it, so physical form emerges from the hidden surge of life. Matter, in this view, is not opposed to life but is its outward effect, a wave thrown up by an inner flame.

couplets.transliteration: giraan garche hai sohbat-e aab o gil / khush aai ise mehnat-e aab o gil
couplets.translation: Though the company of water and clay is a heavy burden, life took delight in the very labour of water and clay.
couplets.explanation: 'Water and clay' (aab o gil) is the classic Persian-Urdu figure for the material body and physical existence. Iqbal concedes that for life-as-spirit, confinement within gross matter is wearisome, a heavy chain. Yet, characteristically, he reverses the lament: life willingly embraces this toil of embodiment, for it is precisely through the struggle with matter that life expresses and develops its powers.

couplets.transliteration: yeh saabit bhi hai aur sayyaar bhi / anaasir ke phandon se bezaar bhi
couplets.translation: Life is at once fixed and ever-moving, and yet weary too of the snares of the elements.
couplets.explanation: Iqbal describes the paradoxical nature of life: it is 'saabit,' constant and unchanging in its essence, while also 'sayyaar,' perpetually in motion and flux, like a fixed star that yet wanders. At the same time life chafes against the 'snares of the elements,' the material constraints of earthly composition that bind it. The couplet holds together permanence and movement, captivity and the longing to transcend, capturing life's restless straining beyond mere matter.

couplets.transliteration: yeh wahdat hai kasrat mein har dam aseer / magar har kaheen be-chaguun be-nazeer
couplets.translation: It is a unity held captive every moment within multiplicity, yet everywhere beyond all 'how' and without parallel.
couplets.explanation: This couplet states one of Iqbal's central metaphysical themes: life is essentially 'wahdat,' a single underlying unity, yet it appears imprisoned within 'kasrat,' the endless multiplicity of created forms. Despite seeming bound by this diversity, that unity remains 'be-chaguun,' beyond the categories of quality and quantity, and 'be-nazeer,' utterly unique and without equal. The verse echoes the Sufi tension between the One and the many while insisting on the transcendence of the underlying reality.

couplets.transliteration: yeh aalam yeh but-khaana-e shash-jihaat / isi ne taraasha hai yeh somnaat
couplets.translation: This world, this idol-temple of the six directions, this Somnath, it is life itself that carved it.
couplets.explanation: Iqbal envisions the universe as a vast 'but-khaana,' an idol-temple extending in all six spatial directions, and names it Somnath, the famed Hindu temple of beautiful idols. The point is that the dazzling multiplicity of forms which the world worships, its very 'idols,' were sculpted by life itself, the single creative principle described in the foregoing couplets. The world of plurality is thus the artwork of the underlying unity, which fashions the many out of itself.

couplets.transliteration: pasand us ko takraar ki khu naheen / ke tu main naheen aur main tu naheen
couplets.translation: Life has no taste for the habit of repetition, for no 'you' is an 'I,' and no 'I' is a 'you.'
couplets.explanation: Life, Iqbal continues, never cares to repeat itself; it is inexhaustibly creative, fashioning each existence as a singular, unrepeatable form. The proof lies in the irreducible distinctness of selves: 'you' is never identical to 'I,' nor 'I' to 'you.' This celebration of individuality flows directly from Iqbal's doctrine of khudi, in which every self is a unique, self-affirming center of being that life refuses to duplicate.

couplets.transliteration: man o tu se hai anjuman-aafareen / magar ain-e mehfil mein khalwat-nasheen
couplets.translation: Through 'I' and 'you' life creates the whole assembly of being, yet in the very midst of the gathering it sits alone in solitude.
couplets.explanation: Out of the distinct selves of 'I' and 'you,' life is the 'anjuman-aafareen,' the creator of the entire society of existence, weaving plurality into a living gathering. And yet, at the heart of that crowded assembly, life remains 'khalwat-nasheen,' dwelling in solitary transcendence, untouched and apart. The couplet resolves the unity-multiplicity theme: the One both generates the many and abides aloof within them, present everywhere yet ultimately alone.

couplets.transliteration: chamak us ki bijli mein tare mein hai / yih chandi mein sone mein pare mein hai
couplets.translation: Its shimmer is in the lightning and in the stars; this same gleam dwells in silver, in gold, and in quicksilver.
couplets.explanation: Iqbal opens the meditation by tracing Life's restless radiance through nature's most luminous things, the flash of lightning and the glitter of stars. He then extends it to the precious metals and to mercury, the ever-trembling element, suggesting that the same vital spark animates and shimmers within all matter. The couplet establishes the central vision: Life (zindagi) is a single shining energy pulsing through every form.

couplets.transliteration: usi ke biyaban usi ke babul / usi ke hain kante usi ke hain phul
couplets.translation: Its are the wildernesses, its the thorn-trees; its alone are the thorns, and its alone the flowers.
couplets.explanation: Life claims ownership of everything, beautiful and harsh alike, the barren deserts and the prickly acacia belong to it no less than the gardens. By the insistent repetition of 'its very own' (usi ke), Iqbal stresses that thorns and flowers spring from a single source. The duality of pain and pleasure, austerity and bloom, is not a contradiction but the full range of one indivisible Life.

couplets.transliteration: kahin us ki taqat se kohsar chur / kahin us ke phande mein Jibril o hur
couplets.translation: Somewhere by its might whole mountain ranges lie crushed; somewhere even Gabriel and the houris are caught in its snare.
couplets.explanation: Iqbal now displays the sheer force of Life: in one place its power pulverizes mountains, in another its subtle snare ensnares even the archangel Gabriel and the celestial houris. The pairing of brute strength with a delicate trap shows that Life conquers both the mightiest matter and the loftiest spiritual beings. Nothing, neither rock nor angel, stands outside its grasp.

words.meaning: a part, a fragment (here: a glimpse/instance)

couplets.transliteration: kahin juzh hai shahin simab rang / lahu se chakoron ke aluda chang
couplets.translation: Somewhere it appears as a quicksilver-hued falcon, its talons smeared with the blood of partridges.
couplets.explanation: Here Life takes the form of the restless, mercury-colored falcon (shaheen), Iqbal's favourite emblem of self-possessed, soaring vitality. The falcon's talons, reddened with the blood of the chakor partridges, dramatize Life as predatory motion, asserting itself by the hunt. The image fuses beauty and violence, showing that Life expresses its energy through ceaseless striving and conquest.

couplets.transliteration: kabutar kahin ashiyane se dur / phaṛakta hua jal mein nasabur
couplets.translation: Somewhere it is a dove, far from its nest, fluttering restlessly, trapped within the net.
couplets.explanation: After the triumphant falcon, Iqbal turns to its opposite: the helpless dove caught far from home, beating its wings impatiently in the snare. This is Life seen as captive struggle, vitality that cannot rest even in capture, ever quivering to break free. Together with the previous couplet it shows Life playing both hunter and hunted, predator and prey, all the while never ceasing to move.

couplets.transliteration: fareb-e-nazar hai sukun o sabat / taṛapta hai har zarra-e kainat
couplets.translation: Stillness and permanence are but a deception of the eye; every single atom of the universe throbs and trembles.
couplets.explanation: This is the philosophical pivot of the passage: what looks like rest and fixity is only an illusion of perception (fareb-e-nazar). In reality not a single atom of the cosmos is at rest, every particle quivers with motion. Iqbal anticipates the modern intuition that all matter is energy in perpetual vibration, grounding his vision of Life as ceaseless dynamism.

couplets.transliteration: ṭhaharta nahin karwan-e vujud / kih har lahza hai taza shan-e vujud
couplets.translation: The caravan of existence never halts, for at every instant Being wears a fresh splendour.
couplets.explanation: Existence is imagined as a caravan that can never stop on its march. The reason follows: at each passing moment Being assumes a new mode and glory (taza shan), echoing the Quranic 'every day He is in a new state' (kulla yawmin huwa fi sha'n). Life and reality are thus perpetual renewal, never repeating, never resting, always becoming.

couplets.transliteration: samajhta hai tu raz hai zindagi / faqat zauq-e parvaz hai zindagi
couplets.translation: You suppose that life is some hidden mystery; but life is nothing other than the sheer zest for flight.
couplets.explanation: Iqbal addresses the reader directly, correcting a common misconception: people imagine Life to be an inscrutable riddle. In truth, he declares, Life is simply the joy and urge of flight (zauq-e parvaz), the innate longing to soar and transcend. Life's essence is not a puzzle to be solved but an impulse to be lived in ceaseless ascent, the very spirit of khudi.

couplets.transliteration: bahut us ne dekhe hain past o buland / safar us ko manzil se baṛh kar pasand
couplets.translation: It has beheld many a depth and many a height; yet it loves the journey far more than the destination.
couplets.explanation: Life is portrayed as a seasoned traveller who has known both the lows and highs of the road. From all this experience it draws one preference: the journey itself is dearer to it than any goal. For Iqbal, arrival means stasis, while travel is living motion, hence Life ever chooses the dynamic path over the static endpoint.

couplets.transliteration: safar zindagi ke liye barg o saz / safar hai haqiqat hazar hai majaz
couplets.translation: Travel is life's very provision and equipage; the journey is the reality, while rest is mere metaphor.
couplets.explanation: Iqbal sharpens the previous thought into a metaphysical maxim. Movement (safar) is the essential outfit and sustenance of Life, without it Life cannot be. Rest or settled dwelling (hazar) is only a figure of speech (majaz), an unreal appearance, whereas the journey is the underlying truth (haqiqat) of all existence.

couplets.transliteration: ulajh kar suljhne mein lazzat use / taṛapne phaṛakne mein rahat use
couplets.translation: It finds delight in getting entangled and then unravelling; it finds repose in throbbing and fluttering.
couplets.explanation: Paradoxically, Life takes pleasure in struggle, first tangling itself in difficulty, then relishing the act of working free. Its very rest is found not in stillness but in palpitation and movement. Iqbal overturns the ordinary idea of comfort: for Life, exertion and quivering effort are themselves the truest ease, because motion is its nature.

words.meaning: to grip, to hold back, withstand

couplets.transliteration: hua jab use samna maut ka / kaṭhin tha baṛa thamna maut ka
couplets.translation: When at last it came face to face with death, to withstand the grip of death was a grievously hard thing.
couplets.explanation: The narrative now turns to Life's great trial: its encounter with death. This confrontation was no small matter, holding one's ground against death proved exceedingly difficult. The couplet sets the stage for the next phase of Iqbal's allegory, in which Life must reckon with mortality as it descends into the worldly order.

couplets.transliteration: utar kar jahan-e mukafat mein / rahi zindagi maut ki ghat mein
couplets.translation: Having descended into the world of requital, life kept lying in wait, in ambush against death.
couplets.explanation: Life now descends into the world of cause and consequence (jahan-e mukafat), the realm where every act meets its requital. There Life does not surrender to death but lies perpetually in ambush against it, watching for its chance. The image transforms the fear of mortality into active resistance: in the world of recompense, Life survives by outwitting and stalking the very death that hunts it.

words.meaning: army (fauj-fauj: in hosts, troop after troop)

couplets.transliteration: mazaq-e dui se bani zauj zauj / uṭhi dasht o kohsar se fauj fauj
couplets.translation: Out of the relish for duality it took shape pair by pair; from desert and mountain it rose up host upon host.
couplets.explanation: From the principle of duality (dui), the disposition toward twoness, creation organized itself into countless pairs (zauj-zauj). Then, teeming life surged from the plains and mountains in host after host (fauj-fauj), multiplying and spreading across the earth. Iqbal shows how the descent into the world of cause and effect unleashes a great proliferation of forms, the abundance of living things arising from polarity.

couplets.transliteration: gul us shakh se ṭuṭte bhi rahe / isi shakh se phuṭte bhi rahe
couplets.translation: Flowers kept breaking off from that branch; and from that very same branch they kept bursting into bloom.
couplets.explanation: Iqbal offers a luminous image of life and death as a single rhythm on one bough. From the same branch flowers are forever falling and forever blossoming anew, withering and budding are two faces of one continuous process. Death does not negate Life; it is part of the perpetual cycle by which the branch keeps renewing itself.

couplets.transliteration: samajhte hain nadan use be-sabat / ubharta hai miṭ miṭ ke naqsh-e hayat
couplets.translation: The ignorant suppose it to be impermanent; yet the design of life keeps re-emerging, again and again after being effaced.
couplets.explanation: Iqbal closes by rebuking the foolish who, seeing things perish, conclude that Life is fleeting and without stability (be-sabat). In truth the very pattern of Life (naqsh-e hayat) reasserts itself precisely by being erased and rising again, effacement is the means of its renewal. What looks like impermanence is the visible rhythm of an indestructible, ever-recurring vitality, the heart of Iqbal's doctrine of perpetual motion and khudi.

couplets.transliteration: baṛī tez jaulāñ baṛī zūd ras / azal se abad tak ram yak nafas
couplets.translation: Very swift in motion, very quick to arrive, from beginning-less eternity to endless eternity it is but the bolting of a single breath.
couplets.explanation: Iqbal opens this central passage by describing time as a force of restless, blazing speed, ceaselessly coursing and instantly reaching its mark. The whole span from azal to abad, the entire arc of eternity, collapses into the dart of one breath, a vivid image preparing the reader for the doctrine that time is nothing but the motion of breaths, and that khudi (selfhood) rides upon this very motion.

couplets.transliteration: zamāna keh zanjīr-e ayyām hai / damoñ ke ulaṭ pher kā nām hai
couplets.translation: Time, which is a chain of days, is merely the name for the turning and returning of breaths.
couplets.explanation: Here Iqbal demystifies time: what seems a fixed chain of successive days is in reality only the rhythmic alternation of breaths, the ceaseless flux of inhalation and exhalation. By reducing time to the pulse of living breath, he removes its tyranny over the self and prepares the claim that khudi, not the clock, is the true measure of reality.

couplets.transliteration: yeh mauj-e nafas kyā hai talwār hai / khudī kyā hai talwār kī dhār hai
couplets.translation: What is this wave of breath? It is a sword. And what is selfhood? It is the cutting edge of that sword.
couplets.explanation: Iqbal converts the breath of time into a weapon: each surging wave of breath is a sword, and khudi is the keen cutting edge upon which all its power is concentrated. This image announces the great theme of the passage, that the self is not passive but the very point of force by which life cleaves through time, the active principle that gives the blade its purpose.

couplets.transliteration: khudī kyā hai rāz-e darūn-e ḥayāt / khudī kyā hai bedārī-e kā'ināt
couplets.translation: What is selfhood? The secret hidden within life. What is selfhood? The very awakening of the universe.
couplets.explanation: Iqbal now defines khudi at its most exalted: it is the inmost secret concealed within life itself, the hidden principle that animates all existence. More than this, it is the awakening, the coming-to-consciousness, of the entire cosmos, as though the universe attains self-awareness through the human self. This couplet places khudi at the metaphysical heart of creation.

couplets.transliteration: khudī jalwa bad-mast o khalwat pasand / samundar hai ik būnd pānī meñ band
couplets.translation: Selfhood is splendour wildly drunk yet solitude-loving, an ocean shut up within a single drop of water.
couplets.explanation: Iqbal captures the paradoxical nature of khudi: it is intoxicated with its own radiant self-display, yet it loves seclusion and inwardness. The second line crystallizes this in a famous image, khudi is an entire ocean compressed into one drop, infinite vastness held within the smallest, most concentrated point. The self thus unites boundless power with utter self-containment.

couplets.transliteration: andhere ujāle meñ hai tābnāk / man o tū meñ paidā man o tū se pāk
couplets.translation: It is luminous in darkness and in light alike; born amid 'I and thou,' yet pure and free of 'I and thou.'
couplets.explanation: Iqbal asserts the self-luminosity of khudi: it shines equally in darkness and in light, depending on no external source. Though it comes into being within the play of 'I and thou', the world of relation and distinction, it transcends that duality, remaining untouched and pure of it. The self both arises from the world of separations and rises above it.

couplets.transliteration: azal us ke pīchhe abad sāmne / na ḥad us ke pīchhe na ḥad sāmne
couplets.translation: Eternity-past lies behind it, eternity-to-come before it; no limit behind it, no limit ahead.
couplets.explanation: Iqbal sets khudi within the infinite stretch of time: the beginningless past trails behind it and the endless future opens before it. There is no boundary on either side, neither in its origin nor in its destination. The self is thus shown to be limitless, partaking of eternity in both directions and never hemmed in by any final frontier.

couplets.transliteration: zamāne ke daryā meñ bahtī huī / sitam us kī maujoñ ke sahtī huī
couplets.translation: Flowing on within the river of time, enduring the cruel blows of its waves.
couplets.explanation: Iqbal pictures khudi as a swimmer in the great river of time, ceaselessly carried along its current. It does not float idly but bears and withstands the harsh battering of time's waves, its tyranny and assaults. This struggle against time's onslaught is precisely how the self proves and tempers its strength, the recurring Iqbalian theme that resistance forges the ego.

couplets.transliteration: tajassus kī rāheñ badaltī huī / dama dam nigāheñ badaltī huī
couplets.translation: Ever changing the paths of its quest, moment by moment shifting its gaze.
couplets.explanation: Iqbal shows khudi as restlessly dynamic in its searching: it constantly alters the routes of its inquiry, never fixed on a single track. From breath to breath it shifts its very gaze, refreshing its vision and reorienting its desire. This perpetual renewal of quest signals that the self is not static but a living, evolving energy that thrives on ceaseless striving.

couplets.transliteration: subuk us ke hāthoñ meñ sang-e girāñ / pahāṛ us kī ẓarboñ se reg-e ravāñ
couplets.translation: In its hands the heaviest stone becomes light; under its blows the mountain turns to shifting sand.
couplets.explanation: Iqbal celebrates the conquering power of khudi: burdens that crush others, heavy stones, weigh nothing in its grip. Whole mountains crumble into drifting sand beneath the force of its strokes. The self, when fully awakened, masters even the most immovable obstacles of nature, transforming what seems permanent and overwhelming into something fluid and pliable to its will.

couplets.transliteration: safar us kā anjām o āghāz hai / yahī us kī taqvīm kā rāz hai
couplets.translation: Its journey is itself its own end and its beginning, this is the very secret of its reckoning of time.
couplets.explanation: Iqbal reveals that for khudi the journey is everything: it is at once its own origin and its own goal, requiring no external destination. The self contains within its very movement both start and finish, so that perpetual travelling is its mode of being. This self-generating motion is the hidden principle behind the self's own measurement of time, its calendar is its ceaseless striving.

couplets.transliteration: kiran chānd meñ hai sharar sang meñ / yeh be-rang hai ḍūb kar rang meñ
couplets.translation: It is the ray within the moon, the spark within the stone; it is colourless, yet plunges itself into colour.
couplets.explanation: Iqbal points to khudi as the latent, hidden energy present everywhere, the beam concealed in the moon, the fire sleeping in the flint. In itself it is colourless and formless, beyond all particular qualities, yet it willingly immerses itself in the world of colour and form. The self manifests through the manifold appearances of creation while remaining, in its essence, pure and undifferentiated.

couplets.transliteration: use vāsta kyā kam o besh se / nashīb o farāz o pas o pesh se
couplets.translation: What has it to do with less and more, with falling and rising, with before and after?
couplets.explanation: Iqbal insists on the sovereign indifference of khudi to all relative measures. It is unconcerned with diminution or increase, with the descents and ascents of fortune, with whatever lies behind or ahead. Standing above the dualities of quantity and direction, the self is anchored in its own absolute being and is not swayed by the fluctuations and oppositions of the contingent world.

couplets.transliteration: azal se hai yeh kashmakash meñ asīr / huī khāk-e ādam meñ sūrat-pazīr
couplets.translation: Since beginning-less eternity it has been a captive of struggle; it took on form within the dust of Adam.
couplets.explanation: Iqbal traces the destiny of khudi back to eternity, where it has always been held prisoner in kashmakash, ceaseless striving and conflict, the very condition by which it grows. This eternal self at last found concrete shape and embodiment in the clay of Adam, in the human being. Thus the cosmic principle of selfhood becomes incarnate in man, who carries within him the struggle that has endured since before time.

couplets.transliteration: khudī kā nashīman tere dil meñ hai / falak jis tarah āñkh ke til meñ hai
couplets.translation: The dwelling-place of selfhood is within your own heart, just as the vast sky is held within the pupil of the eye.
couplets.explanation: Iqbal brings the lofty doctrine home to the individual: the nest of khudi is not in some distant heaven but within your own heart. He illustrates this with a striking image, just as the immense expanse of the sky can be reflected and contained in the tiny pupil of the eye, so the infinite self resides within the small compass of the human heart. The grandeur of selfhood is intimate and inward, available to every person who looks within.

couplets.transliteration: khudī ke nigahbāñ ko hai zahr-e nāb / voh nāñ jis se jātī rahe us kī āb
couplets.translation: To the guardian of selfhood it is sheer, deadly poison, that bread which would cause his lustre and honour to be lost.
couplets.explanation: Iqbal closes the passage with a moral warning to the one who guards his khudi: any sustenance won at the cost of self-respect is pure poison to him. The 'bread' that strips away one's āb, one's dignity, honour, and inner radiance, is more lethal than any toxin, for it destroys the self it was meant to nourish. The keeper of selfhood must therefore reject any gain, however necessary, that humiliates the soul and dims its luminous integrity.

couplets.transliteration: wahi naan hai us ke liye arjumand / rahe jis se dunya mein gardan buland
couplets.translation: That bread alone is worthy for him by which his head stays held high in the world.
couplets.explanation: Iqbal returns to the figure of the seeker's sustenance: the only bread fit for a person of self (khudi) is the bread earned with dignity, which keeps the head erect and the neck unbowed before others. This couplet sets the moral tone for the climactic close of the poem, where honour of the self outweighs mere survival. To eat in a way that humbles you is to betray your khudi.

couplets.transliteration: khudi faal-e-Mahmud se dar-guzar / khudi par nigah rakh Ayazi na kar
couplets.translation: O selfhood, rise above the omen of Mahmud; keep watch over your self and do not play the Ayaz.
couplets.explanation: Iqbal invokes the famous pair of Sultan Mahmud of Ghazni and his beloved slave Ayaz to illustrate the master-servant bond. He tells khudi to transcend even the lure of being a Mahmud and, above all, never to sink into the servile devotion of an Ayaz. The true self keeps vigilant guard over its own dignity and bows to no worldly power.

couplets.transliteration: wahi sajda hai laaiq-e-ehtimaam / ke ho jis se har sajda tujh par haraam
couplets.translation: That prostration alone is worth performing which makes every other prostration forbidden to you.
couplets.explanation: Building on the rejection of servility, Iqbal defines the one act of worship that befits the free self: prostration before God alone. The single true sajda is the one that, once made, renders bowing to any worldly master forbidden. Genuine submission to the Divine is precisely what liberates the self from all idolatry of power.

couplets.transliteration: yeh aalam yeh hangaama-e rang o saut / yeh aalam ke hai zer-e farmaan-e maut
couplets.translation: This world, this tumult of colour and sound, this world that lies under the command of death.
couplets.explanation: Iqbal now turns to the nature of the visible world, calling it a fleeting commotion of colour and sound that dazzles the senses. Yet for all its show, this realm is wholly subject to death's decree. He prepares the seeker to see the material world as transient and not as the final reality of the self.

couplets.transliteration: yeh aalam yeh but-khaana-e chashm o gosh / jahaan zindagi hai faqat khurd o nosh
couplets.translation: This world, this idol-house of the eye and the ear, where life is nothing but eating and drinking.
couplets.explanation: Iqbal sharpens his critique: the sensory world is an idol-house where eye and ear worship the idols of colour and sound. In such a realm, life shrinks to mere eating and drinking, the bare round of bodily appetite. This is the level of existence the awakened self must rise above.

couplets.transliteration: khudi ki yeh hai manzil-e awwaleen / musaafir yeh tera nasheman nahin
couplets.translation: This is only the first stage of selfhood; O traveller, this is not your true abode.
couplets.explanation: Iqbal reveals that the sensory world, for all its hold over the senses, is merely the opening station on khudi's journey. Addressing the seeker as a traveller, he insists this is no resting place or final home. The self is meant to press onward to higher worlds beyond the material.

couplets.transliteration: teri aag us khaak-daan se nahin / jahaan tujh se hai tu jahaan se nahin
couplets.translation: Your fire is not from this handful of dust; the world is from you, you are not from the world.
couplets.explanation: Iqbal proclaims the divine origin of the self: the inner fire of khudi does not arise from the lowly dust of the material world. On the contrary, the world derives its meaning and existence from you, not you from it. The self is the creative centre, the source, never a mere product of matter.

couplets.transliteration: barhe ja yeh koh-e giraan tor kar / tilism-e zamaan o makaan tor kar
couplets.translation: Press ever onward, shattering this heavy mountain, breaking the spell of space and time.
couplets.explanation: Iqbal urges the self to advance relentlessly, smashing through the massive mountain that blocks its path. That obstacle is nothing less than the enchantment of time and space, the limits that bind material existence. The triumphant khudi breaks this spell and moves beyond the cage of the temporal world.

couplets.transliteration: khudi sher-e Maula jahaan us ka said / zameen us ki said aasmaan us ka said
couplets.translation: Selfhood is the Lion of God and the whole world is its prey, earth is its prey, heaven is its prey.
couplets.explanation: Iqbal crowns the self with the title Lion of God (sher-e Maula), echoing the epithet of Ali, the dauntless conqueror. To this lion-self the entire universe is quarry: both earth and heaven fall as its prey. The awakened khudi is no humble captive of the cosmos but its sovereign hunter.

couplets.transliteration: jahaan aur bhi hain abhi be-namood / ke khaali nahin hai zameer-e wujood
couplets.translation: There are still other worlds yet unrevealed, for the conscience of existence is not empty.
couplets.explanation: Iqbal opens an infinite horizon before the self: beyond the visible cosmos lie other worlds not yet brought to manifestation. The reason is that the inner conscience of Being (zameer-e wujood) is never barren or exhausted of possibility. Existence holds endless undisclosed realms awaiting the self's conquest.

couplets.transliteration: har ek muntazir teri yalghaar ka / teri shokhi-e fikr o kirdaar ka
couplets.translation: Each of them awaits your charge, the bold daring of your thought and your action.
couplets.explanation: Iqbal tells the self that every one of those hidden worlds stands waiting for its onslaught. What they await is the seeker's yalghaar, the fearless charge of a self that dares to conquer. Their summons is to the boldness of thought and the audacity of deed by which khudi makes new worlds its own.

couplets.transliteration: yeh hai maqsad-e gardish-e rozgaar / ke teri khudi tujh pe ho aashkaar
couplets.translation: This is the very purpose of the turning of time: that your selfhood be revealed to you.
couplets.explanation: Iqbal discloses the ultimate aim behind the ceaseless revolution of time and fortune. All the turning of the ages serves a single end: that your khudi should at last be unveiled to your own gaze. Self-realization, not the world's spectacle, is the secret goal of the cosmic process.

couplets.transliteration: tu hai faatih-e aalam-e khoob o zisht / tujhe kya bataaun teri sar-nawisht
couplets.translation: You are the conqueror of the world of good and evil, what can I tell you of your destiny!
couplets.explanation: Iqbal hails the self as victor over the whole moral universe of beauty and ugliness, good and evil. So vast is its destiny that the poet confesses he cannot begin to describe what is written on its forehead. The exclamation conveys awe before the boundless fate that awaits the fully awakened khudi.

couplets.transliteration: haqeeqat pe hai jaama-e harf tang / haqeeqat hai aaina-e guftaar zang
couplets.translation: Upon Reality the garment of words is too narrow; to Reality the mirror of speech is but rust.
couplets.explanation: Approaching his close, Iqbal confesses the inadequacy of language before the truth of khudi. The garment of words is too cramped to clothe Reality, and speech, far from reflecting it, dulls it like rust upon a mirror. The deepest reality of the self exceeds all that words can hold or show.

couplets.transliteration: farozaan hai seene mein shama-e nafas / magar taab-e guftaar rakhti hai bas
couplets.translation: The lamp of breath glows aflame within the breast, yet it has only the power of speech and no more.
couplets.explanation: Iqbal extends the confession of language's limits to his own poetic breath. The candle of his breath burns bright within his bosom, yet its strength reaches only as far as speech. It can articulate but cannot fully reveal the Reality it longs to express, words can illumine, but only so far.

words.meaning: a hair (sar-e mooy = a hair's breadth)

couplets.transliteration: agar yak sar-e mooy bartar param / farogh-e tajalli bisozad param
couplets.translation: If I should fly even a hair's breadth higher, the radiance of the Divine theophany would burn my wings.
couplets.explanation: Iqbal seals the poem with a celebrated Persian verse, the words attributed to the angel Gabriel at the limit of the Prophet's Mi'raj, who declares that to ascend a single hair's breadth further would set his wings ablaze in the Divine effulgence. By quoting this Persian line he marks the boundary beyond which even the most exalted seeker's vision cannot endure the glory of God. The switch from Urdu to this famous Persian couplet is itself an act of humility, closing the discourse on khudi at the very threshold where speech and sight alike must halt before the Absolute.
